All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#4184.body:
**What problem or use case are you trying to solve?**

Not Diamond intelligently identifies which LLM is best-suited to respond to any given query. We want to implement a mechanism in OpenHands to support this type of "LLM" selector.

**Describe the UX of the solution you'd like**

Ideally, use should define a "LLMRouter" as a special type of LLM with some special configs (e.g., multiple keys for different providers). And user can just put in keys, and select that router, and OpenHands will automatically use that going forward.

**Do you have thoughts on the technical implementation?**

Modify https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/blob/main/openhands/llm/llm.py, as well as config related files under https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/tree/main/openhands/core/config.

You should probably use `model_select` (from notdiamond API) rather than `create` to be compatible with existing LiteLLM calls.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

**Additional context**

Here's the documentation from NotDiamond

```
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
"""Getting started with Not Diamond

Automatically generated by Colab.

Original file is located at
    https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1Ao-YhYF_S6QP5UGp_kYhgKps_Sw3a2RO

# **Setting up**
"""

!pip install -q notdiamond[create] --upgrade

import os

os.environ["NOTDIAMOND_API_KEY"] = 'YOUR_NOTDIAMOND_API_KEY'
os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = 'YOUR_OPENAI_API_KEY'
os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"] = 'YOUR_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY'
os.environ["PPLX_API_KEY"] = 'YOUR_PERPLEXITY_API_KEY'

"""# **Automatic routing and model calling**

We'll start by defining the routing `NotDiamond` client, which will function like a 'meta-LLM' that ensembles together the best of multiple models.

We then add specific LLM targets for routing. Not Diamond works with any LLM in [this list](https://notdiamond.readme.io/docs/llm-models).
"""

from notdiamond import NotDiamond

client = NotDiamond()

llm_providers = [
    'openai/gpt-4o',
    'anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620',
    'openai/gpt-4o-mini',
    'perplexity/llama-3.1-sonar-large-128k-online'
]

"""Next, let's call the client by passing in an array of messages and our target models:"""

result, session_id, provider = client.chat.completions.create(
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Concisely explain merge sort."}  # Adjust as desired
    ],
  	model=llm_providers
)

print("LLM called: \n", provider.model)
print("\nLLM output: \n", result.content)

"""If we pass in a different question, `NotDiamond` will recommend a different model:"""

result, session_id, provider = client.chat.completions.create(
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "What's the weather like in SF today?"}
    ],
  	model=llm_providers
)

print("LLM called: \n", provider.model)
print("\nLLM output: \n", result.content)

"""# **Defining tradeoffs**

We can also define tradeoffs for cost or latency to adjust routing to our preferences:
"""

result, session_id, provider = client.chat.completions.create(
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Concisely explain merge sort."}
    ],
  	model=llm_providers,
  	tradeoff="cost" # Consider cheaper models when quality loss is negligible. Alternatively, you can use "latency".
)

print("LLM called: \n", provider.model)
print("\nLLM output: \n", result.content)

"""# **Routing recommendations with `model_select`**

Finally, we can also use `model_select` to return a recommended LLM for your prompt. You can then invoke that LLM using your own custom logic.
"""

session_id, provider = client.chat.completions.model_select(
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Concisely explain merge sort."}
    ],
  	model=llm_providers
)

## Add more application logic here. e.g.:

print(f"LLM called: \n{provider.model}\n", )

match provider.model:
    case "claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620":
        print("Running custom_sonnet_3-5_invoke()...")
        # custom_sonnet_35_invoke()
    case 'gpt-4o':
        print("Running custom_gpt_4o_invoke()...")
        # custom_gpt_4o_invoke()
    case 'llama-3-sonar-large-32k-online':
        print("Running custom_pplx_llama3_invoke()...")
        # custom_pplx_llama3_invoke()
    case 'gpt-4o-mini':
        print("Running custom_gpt_4o_mini_invoke()...")
        # custom_gpt_4o_mini_invoke()
```
All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#4184.title: [LLM] Support LLM routing

### Is there an existing issue for the same bug? (If one exists, thumbs up or comment on the issue instead). - [X] I have checked the existing issues. ### Describe the bug and reproduction steps When running OpenHands on a remote machine accessed via Tailscale (or similar),
the browser tries to connect to localhost ports instead of using the correct
remote IP address. Steps to reproduce: 1. Set up a remote machine with OpenHands 2. Configure with DOCKER_HOST_ADDR= (e.g., 100.121.70.106) 3. Run OpenHands using:
docker run -it --rm --pull=always
-e SANDBOX_RUNTIME_CONTAINER_IMAGE="docker.all-hands.dev/all-hands-ai/runtime:0.
37-nikolaik"
-e SANDBOX_USER_ID="$(id -u)"
-e SANDBOX_VOLUMES="${APP_PATH}:/workspace:rw"
-e VITE_BACKEND_BASE_URL="100.121.70.106:3000"
-e VITE_BACKEND_HOST="100.121.70.106:3000"
-e DOCKER_HOST_ADDR="100.121.70.106"
-e SANDBOX_RUNTIME_BINDING_ADDRESS="100.121.70.106"
-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
-p 3000:3000
--name openhand-local
docker.all-hands.dev/all-hands-ai/openhands:0.37 4. Access the OpenHands UI at [http://100.121.70.106:3000/]() 5. Observe in browser network tab: requests to localhost ports failing with
ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED Expected behavior:
All network requests should use the configured DOCKER_HOST_ADDR (100.121.70.106)
instead of localhost. Current behavior:
Browser console shows errors like:
GET [http://localhost:55400/]() net::ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED
GET [http://localhost:53254/]() net::ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED ### OpenHands Installation Docker command in README ### OpenHands Version 0.37 ### Operating System Linux ### Logs, Errors, Screenshots, and Additional Context *No response* Description of the fix: File to modify: openhands/runtime/impl/docker/docker_runtime.py ``` @property def web_hosts(self): hosts: dict[str, int] = {} # Use DOCKER_HOST_ADDR if defined, otherwise use localhost host_addr = os.environ.get('DOCKER_HOST_ADDR', 'localhost') for port in self._app_ports: hosts[f'http://{host_addr}:{port}'] = port return hosts ``` I could not verify the fix as I face issue building the docker image for local use
I am not sure about vscode_url, maybe need to do the same change (VS Code was working for me with tailscale)

### Describe the bug and reproduction steps

Whenever I try to use OpenHands in CLI mode with Docker, it always asks about LLM settings.

Here is my `config.toml` file

```toml
[core]
search_api_key = "tvly-***"
reasoning_effort = "high"
platform = "linux/amd64" # Can be linux/amd64 or linux/arm64

[llm]
api_key = "sk-proj-***"
# API base URL (For Headless / CLI only -  In Web this is overridden by Session Init)
#base_url = ""
provider = "openai" # Can be openai, anthropic, deepseek, openrouter
# Model to use. (For Headless / CLI only -  In Web this is overridden by Session Init)
model = "openai/o3"
```

Here is the command that I used to run OpenHands CLI mode with Docker:

```bash
docker run -it --rm --pull=always \
        -e SANDBOX_RUNTIME_CONTAINER_IMAGE=docker.all-hands.dev/all-hands-ai/runtime:0.43.0-nikolaik \
        -e LOG_ALL_EVENTS=true \
        -e SANDBOX_USER_ID=$(id -u) \
        -e SANDBOX_VOLUMES=$SANDBOX_VOLUMES \
        -e RUNTIME_MOUNT=$RUNTIME_MOUNT \
        -e LLM_API_KEY=$LLM_API_KEY \
        -e LLM_PROVIDER=$LLM_PROVIDER \
        -e LLM_MODEL=$LLM_MODEL \
        -e AGENT_MEMORY_ENABLED=$AGENT_MEMORY_ENABLED \
        -e LLM_CACHING_PROMPT=$LLM_CACHING_PROMPT \
        -e AGENT_ENABLE_THINK=$AGENT_ENABLE_THINK \
        -e LLM_NUM_RETRIES=$LLM_NUM_RETRIES \
        -e AGENT_ENABLE_MCP=$AGENT_ENABLE_MCP \
        -e CORE_PLATFORM=$CORE_PLATFORM \
        -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock \
        -v ~/.openhands-state:/.openhands-state \
        -v $RUNTIME_MOUNT \
        -w /workspace \
        -p 3080:3080 \
        --add-host host.docker.internal:host-gateway \
        --network oha-network \
        --name "${CONTAINER_NAME}" \
        docker.all-hands.dev/all-hands-ai/openhands:0.43.0 \
        python3 -m openhands.cli.main --override-cli-mode true
```

Here is the result

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7488a279-05e7-48a6-9625-1b342d8fcf48)

Expected behaviour: If we have already specified the configurations in the `config.toml` file or via environment variables, we shouldn't ask for it again when starting OpenHands.

### OpenHands Installation

Docker command in README

### OpenHands Version

v0.42, v0.43

### Operating System

macOS

All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#9344.body:
## Bug Description

When running OpenHands from CLI with a custom `config.toml` that sets `max_iterations = 1000`, the system still stops execution at 500 iterations (the default limit) with an iteration limit error.

## Environment
- **Installation**: Development version installed with `pip install openhands -e .`
- **Command**: `openhands -n vscode-runtime` (run from project directory)
- **Config**: Custom `config.toml` with `max_iterations = 1000`
- **Verification**: Other config options from the same file are being respected

## Expected Behavior
The system should respect the `max_iterations = 1000` setting from `config.toml` and allow up to 1000 iterations before stopping.

## Actual Behavior
The system stops at 500 iterations with an iteration limit error, ignoring the custom configuration.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Create/modify `config.toml` with `max_iterations = 1000`
2. Install development version: `pip install openhands -e .`
3. Run: `openhands -n vscode-runtime`
4. Observe that execution stops at 500 iterations despite config setting

## Additional Context
- The config file is definitely being read (other settings are applied)
- This affects workflows where longer sessions are needed

---
*Reported by: OpenHands-Claude (AI Assistant)*
All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#9344.title: Bug: [CLI] Iteration limit enforced at default despite config.toml setting max_iterations=1000

All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#9354.body:
**What problem or use case are you trying to solve?**

OpenHands initially supported GitHub, then in issues #5210 and #7080 we added support for GitLab. Several users on those issues have now requested that we extend the platform integrations to also cover Gitea and Forgejo.

**Describe the UX or technical implementation you have in mind**

I believe we can leverage a similar approach to what was done for the GitLab integration. The key changes required would be:

1. **API Client**: Implement a new API client module to interact with the Gitea and Forgejo APIs. This should mirror the existing GitHub and GitLab clients.
2. **Authentication**: Extend the PAT system (#7080) to support Gitea tokens
3. **Resolver**: Update the resolver to support the new API client
4. **Workflows**: Create a new workflow definition similar to `.github/workflows/openhands-resolver.yml`

**Additional context**

For more technical information see the comment by @malhotra5 in [#5210](https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/issues/5210#issuecomment-2498698884).


### If you find this feature request or enhancement useful, make sure to add a 👍 to the issue
All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#9354.title: Gitea/Forgejo Support

All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#9385.body:
## Problem
When modifying advanced settings in the CLI, users cannot change individual settings. Instead, they must replace all settings one-by-one, even if they only want to change a single value.

## Current Behavior
- `/settings` → Advanced → User must input all settings again
- Cannot selectively modify just one setting (e.g., only the API key)
- Forces users to re-enter all configuration values

## Expected Behavior
- Allow selective modification of individual settings
- Show current values and allow users to keep existing values
- Provide options like "Keep current value" or show defaults

## User Experience Impact
- Frustrating when only wanting to change one setting
- Risk of introducing errors when re-entering all values
- Time-consuming for users with complex configurations

## Technical Context
The issue is in the settings modification flow in `openhands/cli/settings.py`, specifically in the `modify_llm_settings_advanced()` function.

## Proposed Solutions
1. **Interactive setting selection**: Allow users to choose which settings to modify
2. **Current value display**: Show existing values and allow keeping them
3. **Incremental updates**: Only prompt for settings the user wants to change

## Related Files
- `openhands/cli/settings.py` (modify_llm_settings_advanced function)
- `openhands/cli/commands.py` (handle_settings_command function)
All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#9385.title: [CLI] Advanced settings modification requires replacing all settings one-by-one

All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#9388.body:
## Problem
The CLI displays full command output regardless of length, which can overwhelm the terminal with very long outputs and make it difficult to follow the conversation.

## Current Behavior
- All command output is displayed in full
- Long outputs (hundreds of lines) fill the entire terminal
- Makes it hard to see the conversation flow
- Wastes screen real estate

## Expected Behavior
- Show only the first 10-15 lines of command output by default
- Display a count of remaining lines (e.g., "... and 150 more lines")
- Provide option to view full output when requested
- Better use of terminal space

## User Experience Impact
- **Readability**: Long outputs make conversation hard to follow
- **Navigation**: Difficult to scroll back to see previous interactions
- **Focus**: Important information gets lost in verbose output

## Proposed Solution
```
Command Output (showing 15 of 200 lines)
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│ line 1 of output                    │
│ line 2 of output                    │
│ ...                                 │
│ line 15 of output                   │
│ ... and 185 more lines             │
│ (use --full to see complete output) │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Technical Context
The truncation logic should be added to `display_command_output()` in `openhands/cli/tui.py`.

## Related Files
- `openhands/cli/tui.py` (display_command_output function)
All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#9388.title: [CLI] Command output should be truncated with line count for better space usage

All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#10013.body:
## Problem

OpenHands currently has unpredictable Git commit, push, and create PR/MR behavior that varies depending on the interface being used. This inconsistency creates confusion for users and makes the system behavior unpredictable.

## Current Inconsistent Behavior

### GUI Mode
- **Current**: If user doesn't ask the agent to push or create PR, the agent should NOT do it
- **Issue**: This behavior is not consistently enforced

### Cloud Resolver
- **Current**: Even if the user doesn't ask the agent to push, it should by DEFAULT work on the exact same branch and push to the exact same branch when it is done
- **Issue**: This automatic behavior is inconsistent with GUI mode

### Slack Integration
- **Current**: The agent SHOULD explicitly ask user in the end whether they want to create a NEW PR
- **Issue**: This interactive approach differs from other interfaces

## Proposed Solution

### Recommended Implementation Approach

Add trigger information in the `additional_info.j2` template to inform the agent about the expected default behavior based on how the conversation was initiated:

1. **Modify `additional_info.j2`** to include context about the conversation trigger (GUI, GitHub Resolver, Slack, etc.)
2. **Define clear behavioral expectations** for each trigger type within the template
3. **Remove conflicting PR-related instructions** from the system prompt to reduce confusion
4. **Centralize Git behavior logic** in the additional info template rather than scattered across different prompts

### Interface-Specific Behaviors

#### 1. GUI Mode
- **Behavior**: Conservative approach - never push or create PRs unless explicitly requested
- **Rationale**: Users have full control and can see what's happening
- **Template Context**: `trigger_type: "gui", auto_push: false, auto_pr: false`

#### 2. GitHub Resolver
- **Behavior**: Automatic push to the same branch when work is complete
- **Rationale**: This is typically used for automated issue resolution where the expectation is that work will be committed
- **Template Context**: `trigger_type: "github_resolver", auto_push: true, auto_pr: false`
- **Consideration**: Should still respect repository permissions and branch protection rules

#### 3. Slack Integration
- **Behavior**: Always ask for user confirmation before creating PRs
- **Rationale**: Slack users may not have immediate visibility into the changes and should have explicit control
- **Template Context**: `trigger_type: "slack", auto_push: false, auto_pr: "ask_user"`

## Implementation Requirements

1. **Update `additional_info.j2` template** to include trigger context and behavioral expectations
2. **Clean up system prompts** by removing conflicting PR-related instructions
3. **Add clear documentation** explaining the different behaviors for each interface
4. **Implement consistent logging** so users understand what Git operations are being performed
5. **Add user preferences** where appropriate to override default behaviors
6. **Ensure proper error handling** for Git operations across all interfaces

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `additional_info.j2` template includes trigger type and behavioral context
- [ ] System prompts are cleaned up to remove conflicting PR instructions

## Technical Implementation Notes

- The `additional_info.j2` template is likely located in the prompt templates directory
- Need to identify where trigger type information is available in the conversation context
- May need to update conversation initialization logic to pass trigger type to the template
All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#10013.title: Standardize Git commit, push, and PR/MR behavior across different OpenHands interfaces

All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#10115.body:
**What problem or use case are you trying to solve?**

We should have end-to-end tests implemented as a github workflow that can be optionally triggered with a label "end-to-end". They would do the following:

1. Assume that we have GITHUB_TOKEN, LLM_MODEL, LLM_API_KEY, and possibly LLM_BASE_URL set in the environment.
2. Start OpenHands according to the development workflow (using `make build; make run`).
3. Use playwright to manipulate the interface to:
   a. Click on the "All-Hands-AI/OpenHands" repo in the "Select a repo" dropdown
   b. Click "Launch"
   c. Check that the interface changes to the interface where we can control the agent
   d. Check that we go through the "Connecting", "Initializing Agent", and "Agent is waiting for user input..." states
   e. Enter "How many lines are there in the main [README.md](http://README.md) file?" and click the submit button
   f. Check that we go through the "Agent is running task" and "Agent has finished the task." states
   h. Check that the final agent message contains a number that matches "wc -l [README.md](http://README.md)"

In implementing this you may need to run several long-running programs. When you do so, run them in the background and pipe the logs to a file, and run `sleep XXX` with the appropriate timeout set in `execute_bash`. Note that you will have to install docker and set up the docker daemon, which will also take time.

Working doc: [https://docs.google.com/document/d/16HNKUdoKH-7WbmHDvhkYelnIZ9nmg5ocFoHVlQA03p0/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.lu354lht1en7](https://docs.google.com/document/d/16HNKUdoKH-7WbmHDvhkYelnIZ9nmg5ocFoHVlQA03p0/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.lu354lht1en7)

### If you find this feature request or enhancement useful, make sure to add a 👍 to the issue

All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#10158.body:
**What problem or use case are you trying to solve?**

We've noticed that DeepSeek-R1-0528 and other reasoning LLMs will put `<function></function>` tags in their `reasoning_content` as they attempt to work through their next steps. This immediately halts the conversation and makes it appear as if the `<think>` tag never closed and there is no `content` property in the result.

Some of these models should probably be added here: https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/blob/3a629cdf081cc759b006952c2ba8335251d1bf87/openhands/llm/llm.py#L112

However, naming is hit or miss with third party API providers and if the user is using a local LLM via `llama.cpp` they could name the model "fluffy bunny 123" if they want. We need a more reliable way to allow the user to disabled the `stop` property in the request.

**Describe the UX or technical implementation you have in mind**

We currently have toggle switched in the Advanced LLM dialog. I suggest adding another toggle switch for `Reasoning Model` that will disabled the `stop` request property when "checked". We could also name it `stop` or `no stop`. I'm not great at naming things. I just feel like `Reasoning Model` might be more flexible moving forward.

**Additional context**

This also affects:
- https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/issues/8218
- https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/pull/9374
- https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/issues/9370


### If you find this feature request or enhancement useful, make sure to add a 👍 to the issue

All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#10346.body:
### Is there an existing issue for the same bug? (If one exists, thumbs up or comment on the issue instead).

- [x] I have checked the existing issues.

### Describe the bug and reproduction steps

docker pull docker.all-hands.dev/all-hands-ai/runtime:0.53-nikolaik

docker run -it --rm --pull=always \
    -e SANDBOX_RUNTIME_CONTAINER_IMAGE=docker.all-hands.dev/all-hands-ai/runtime:0.53-nikolaik \
    -e SANDBOX_USER_ID=$(id -u) \
    -e LLM_API_KEY=$OPEN_API_KEY \
    -e LLM_MODEL=openai/gpt-5 \
    -e LOG_ALL_EVENTS=true \
    -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock \
    -v "$STATE_DIR:/.openhands:rw" \
    -p 3000:3000 \
    --add-host host.docker.internal:host-gateway \
    --name "$CONTAINER_NAME" \
    docker.all-hands.dev/all-hands-ai/openhands:0.53 \
    python -m openhands.core.main -t "what is the capital of the moon?"


The issue persists when running with a LITELLM_KEY instead of an OPENAI_API_KEY (and correspondingly the ANTHROPIC_API_KEY for the claude 4.1 model ('anthropic/claude-opus-4-1-20250805'))


Error for OpenAI GPT-5 model ('openai/gpt-5'):
[Agent Controller ec9be6da-6f6e-4f-4559d33b96ef28f] AgentStateChangedObservation(content='', agent_state='error', reason="BadRequestError: litellm.BadRequestError: OpenAIException - Unsupported value: 'temperature' does not support 0.0 with this model. Only the default (1) value is supported.", observation=<ObservationType.AGENT_STATE_CHANGED: 'agent_state_changed'>)

Error for Anthropic Claude 4.1 model ('anthropic/claude-opus-4-1-20250805'):

Please use only one.\\"}}. Received Model Group=claude-opus-4-1-20250805\\nAvailable Model Group Fallbacks=None","type":"None","param":"None","code":"400"}}', observation=<ObservationType.AGENT_STATE_CHANGED: 'agent_state_changed'>)

[I also tried LLM_DROP_PARAMS=True and that didn't work either]

All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#10523.body:
The `create_pr`/`create_mr` tool tells the LLM to add labels. GPT-5 seems very strict/sensitive to prompts, it tries to do whatever you said :melting_face: , and... it adds plenty of labels, inventing them
• so we added [an instruction](https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/pull/10446) to make it stop inventing them (so use existing)
• should we actually move that text in the tool description, rather than the microagents? Same place where we instruct it to apply labels.

[Slack Message](https://openhands-ai.slack.com/archives/C086ARSNMGA/p1755698385551819)
All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#10523.title: Tweak labels prompt

All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#10592.body:
**What problem or use case are you trying to solve?**
I am trying to setup a local proxy https server using self-signed certificates. This allows me to create a sandboxed http/https environment to reliably test some browser agent action.

I was able to get curl to accept the local proxy, yet when visiting the same HTTPS URL, I get the following from agent observation:

```
URL: chrome-error://chromewebdata/
Error: True
Open pages: ['chrome-error://chromewebdata/']
Active page index: 0
Last browser action: goto('https://www.airbnb.com/')
Last browser action error: Error: Page.goto: net::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID at https://www.airbnb.com/
Call log:
  - navigating to "https://www.airbnb.com/", waiting until "load"
```

**Describe the UX or technical implementation you have in mind**
Probably some option that allows Playwright controlled by browser-gym to ignore SSL errors?

**Additional context**


### If you find this feature request or enhancement useful, make sure to add a 👍 to the issue
All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#10592.title: [Browser agent]: Support ignore SSL error

All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#10684.body:
**What problem or use case are you trying to solve?**

Some tools take a long time to complete. Being able to increase the timeout can help avoid failures due to long processes.

**Describe the UX or technical implementation you have in mind**

Add a timeout option to the mcp servers options e.g. `mcp.shttp_servers[0].timeout = 60`

**Additional context**


### If you find this feature request or enhancement useful, make sure to add a 👍 to the issue
All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#10684.title: Add timeout for mcp tools

All-Hands-AI/OpenHands#11206.body:
### Is there an existing issue for the same bug? (If one exists, thumbs up or comment on the issue instead).

- [x] I have checked the existing issues.

### Describe the bug and reproduction steps

I'm running locally via Docker, and have set up access to local filesystem[1]. Whenever a new conversation is started, a git repo is created (don't want it, but that's only a nuisance), and the folder is chowned to `<openhands-user>:root`. I don't see any option/environment variable to set the group for the chown, nor to stop creating the git repo.

This also means that any file the agent creates is owned by group `root`.

Current workaround:
When creating a new conversation, run `sudo chown -R $UID:$GID .`, and also whenever the agent has created any files I need to modify.


Command I use to start:
```bash
export OPENHANDS_VERSION=0.57
export OPENHANDS_RUNTIME=docker.all-hands.dev/all-hands-ai/runtime:$OPENHANDS_VERSION-nikolaik
export OPENHANDS_IMAGE=docker.all-hands.dev/all-hands-ai/openhands:$OPENHANDS_VERSION
export OPENHANDS_USER=$(echo "$DOCKER_HOST" | grep podman && echo root || echo $UID)

docker rm -f openhands-app &>/dev/null
docker run -it \
  --rm \
  --add-host host.docker.internal:host-gateway \
  --name openhands-app \
  --security-opt label=disable \
  -e LOG_ALL_EVENTS=true \
  -e LOG_LEVEL=debug \
  -e SANDBOX_RUNTIME_CONTAINER_IMAGE=$OPENHANDS_RUNTIME \
  -e SANDBOX_GROUP_ID=$OPENHANDS_USER \
  -e SANDBOX_USER_ID=$OPENHANDS_USER \
  -e SANDBOX_VOLUMES=$PWD:/workspace:Z \
  -p 127.0.0.1:3000:3000 \
  -v ${DOCKER_HOST#*://}:/var/run/docker.sock \
  -v ~/.openhands:/.openhands \
  $OPENHANDS_IMAGE
```



 [1]: https://docs.all-hands.dev/usage/runtimes/docker#using-sandbox-volumes

All-Hands-AI/OpenHands-Cloud#224.body:
On the OpenHands Cloud, GITHUB_TOKEN variables expire and are not renewed.

This causes the agent to not be able to push code, be confused, etc.

* A customer reported this happened after working with the agent for 60+ minutes in GitLab. We have also heard similar issues with GitHub. 
* A customer reported that OpenHands was unable to push to Bitbucket and it wasn't able to use the Oauth token.

Reference links:

* [Bitbucket OAuth access tokens expire in 2 hours](https://community.atlassian.com/forums/Bitbucket-questions/Re-Git-push-using-OAuth-2-0-token-is-not-working-quot-remot/qaq-p/2650857/comment-id/102940#M102940)
* [GitLab OAuth access tokens expire in 2 hours](https://forum.gitlab.com/t/can-refresh-tokens-expire-for-gitlab-oauth/64886)
All-Hands-AI/OpenHands-Cloud#224.title: [Bug] GITHUB_TOKEN (and other providers e.g., GitLab) expire and are not renewed on resume

OpenHands/OpenHands#11764.body:
## Description

The favicon displayed in Chrome tabs is not square, which causes the icon to be cut off. This affects the visual appearance of the site in the browser tab.

## Current Behavior

The favicon appears cut off in Chrome browser tabs due to the non-square aspect ratio.

## Proposed Solution

Add padding to the top and bottom of the favicon to make it square. This will ensure the icon displays properly without being cut off in Chrome tabs.

## Additional Context

This issue was observed in the staging environment and affects the Chrome browser specifically.
OpenHands/OpenHands#11764.title: Chrome favicon is not square causing it to be cut off

OpenHands/OpenHands#12049.body:
**What problem or use case are you trying to solve?**
Currently, getting the OpenHands agent to use the 'App' tab in the GUI is very difficult. Most users do not know the exact prompts to use to ensure the web server displays the app in this tab. When it is working, the experience is better for the end user because they can interact directly with the web app, rather than rely on screenshots from the browser.

**Describe the UX or technical implementation you have in mind**
- Understand the prompts or steps required by the user to get a  web app to appear in the 'App' tab. Document this.
- Improve the OpenHands agent, or suggest a feature, that allows users to load web apps into the 'App' tab more reliably and predictably
- Change the name of this from 'App' to 'Web App' if that's the type of application that can be displayed here


### If you find this feature request or enhancement useful, make sure to add a 👍 to the issue
OpenHands/OpenHands#12049.title: [App tab] Improve ability for Agent to use the App tab

OpenHands/OpenHands#12394.body:
## Background
There is confusion and inconsistent behavior around “Available Skills” in the OpenHands UI:

- Users expect “global/public skills” to be loaded from the dedicated repository: https://github.com/OpenHands/skills/tree/main/skills
- Current SaaS UI/app-server behavior for v1 conversations loads skills from:
  - `OpenHands/skills/` inside the OpenHands monorepo (treated as “global skills”)
  - org-level config repo (`.openhands` / `openhands-config`)
  - repo-level `.openhands/skills` (and `.openhands/microagents` for v0/back-compat)
  - user `~/.openhands/skills`

Per discussion, the agent-server / SDK already supports loading “public/global skills” from the new repository, but OpenHands app-server does not consistently rely on that and instead re-implements loading/merge logic.

This results in cases like the Notion skill existing in `OpenHands/skills` repo but not appearing in UI “Available Skills”.

## Problem Statement
1. The OpenHands app-server currently loads/merges skills itself rather than delegating to agent-server/SDK.
2. “Global skills” in app-server are assumed to come from `OpenHands/skills/` (monorepo) rather than the dedicated `OpenHands/skills` repo.
3. Frontend “Available Skills” can diverge from CLI/SDK behavior (`load_public_skills`/`load_user_skills` expectations).

## Goals
- Make the SDK/agent-server the canonical resolver for the full skills set (including public/global skills from the `OpenHands/skills` repo).
- Make the OpenHands app-server a thin layer that:
  1) requests resolved skills from agent-server/SDK and
  2) exposes them to frontend via REST.
- Ensure the UI “Tools → show available skills” reflects the same skill set the agent actually uses.

## Non-goals
- Reworking skill formats/triggers unless required for compatibility.
- Copying public skills into the OpenHands monorepo as a workaround.

## Current Implementation Notes (OpenHands repo)
- App-server endpoint exists: `GET /{conversation_id}/skills` in `openhands/app_server/app_conversation/app_conversation_router.py`.
- It calls `AppConversationServiceBase.load_and_merge_all_skills(...)`.
- The loader currently treats “global skills” as local monorepo `OpenHands/skills/` (not `OpenHands/skills` GitHub repo).

## Proposed Implementation
### A) Standardize a resolved skills API in agent-server (preferred)
Add or standardize an agent-server endpoint (illustrative):
- `GET /skills` or `GET /v1/skills`

It should return resolved skills plus metadata (source: public/global/user/org/repo/sandbox), triggers, content, etc.

Agent-server should:
- load public/global skills from `OpenHands/skills` repo (cached with refresh semantics)
- load user/org/repo skills as currently supported
- merge with deterministic precedence rules
- return the final resolved list

### B) Update OpenHands app-server to call agent-server for skills
In OpenHands app-server:
- Replace direct loading/merge logic in:
  - `openhands/app_server/app_conversation/app_conversation_service_base.py` (`load_and_merge_all_skills`)
  - `openhands/app_server/app_conversation/skill_loader.py`
  with a client call to agent-server’s resolved-skills endpoint.

Keep `GET /{conversation_id}/skills` but make it proxy/translate the agent-server response into the UI response model.

### C) Backward compatibility
- Continue supporting `.openhands/microagents` and `.openhands/skills` (repo-level) as agent-server already does.
- Keep v0 conversation behavior unchanged; v1 uses unified agent-server resolver.

### D) Caching / “Sync” semantics
Clarify behavior in code + UI:
- whether “Sync” triggers cache refresh in agent-server
- expected TTL/refresh behavior for public skills repo fetch
- ensure UI updates after sync

## Acceptance Criteria
- A skill added to `OpenHands/skills` repo (e.g., `skills/notion/...`) appears in OpenHands UI “Available Skills” without copying into `OpenHands/OpenHands` monorepo.
- Skills returned to UI match skills available to the agent during execution.
- Only one component (agent-server/SDK) owns merge logic and precedence rules.
- Refresh/sync behavior is documented and predictable.
OpenHands/OpenHands#12394.title: Use agent-server/SDK as source of truth for skills loading (incl. OpenHands/skills repo) and expose resolved skills via app-server API

OpenHands/OpenHands#13275.body:
# RFC: OpenHands Automations — Scheduled & Event-Driven Conversations **Authors**: Xingyao, Joe
**Date**: March 2026
**Status**: Draft — for team discussion
**Related issues**: * [#12732]() — PRD: Scheduled, Recurring Conversations * [#13272]() — Bug: SDK conversations invisible in Cloud UI (prerequisite) * [#13268]() — SDK conversations should inherit SaaS settings (prerequisite) --- ## 1\. Motivation Users want OpenHands to run tasks automatically — on a schedule, or in response to events like a PR being opened or a Datadog alert firing. Today this requires wiring up GitHub Actions with SDK scripts (see [software-agent-sdk#2341]() for a concrete example), which is painful: * Every repo needs its own workflow YAML, upgraded individually * Conversations run outside Cloud with no dashboard visibility ([#13272]()) * Users must supply their own credentials instead of using their Cloud account ([#13268]()) * Extending beyond GitHub (Slack, Datadog, PagerDuty) means building new pipelines each time Automations are a key differentiator for the control plane and we should move quickly. ## 2\. Design Principle: SDK Code as the Universal Execution Unit Every automation — whether the user typed a prompt, selected a plugin, or uploaded a custom script — is **represented as SDK code**. The platform only has one execution path: run a Python script in a Cloud sandbox. Why: * **One system to build and maintain**, not separate paths for prompts vs. plugins vs. scripts * **Natural SDK education**: the UI generates code from user selections; users can "View Source" and customize * **Maximum flexibility**: anything expressible in SDK code (multi-step workflows, conditional logic, multiple conversations) just works ### 2a. Prompt-based automation A user fills out a form in the Cloud UI: picks a repo, writes a prompt ("Summarize open PRs from this week"), and sets a schedule. The platform generates the SDK code behind the scenes: ```python agent = Agent(llm=llm, tools=get_default_tools()) conversation = Conversation(agent=agent, workspace="/workspace/project") conversation.send_message("Summarize open PRs from this week") conversation.run() ``` The user can click "View Source" to see this code and optionally edit it. ### 2b. Plugin-based automation A user browses the plugin marketplace, selects a plugin (e.g., "PR Review"), configures its parameters, and sets a trigger (e.g., "when a PR is labeled `review-this`"). The platform generates: ```python agent = Agent( llm=llm, tools=get_default_tools(), plugins=[PluginSource(source="github:OpenHands/pr-review-plugin")], ) conversation = Conversation(agent=agent, workspace="/workspace/project") conversation.send_message("Review PR #42") conversation.run() ``` Same "View Source" escape hatch — the user can customize the generated code. ### 2c. Custom script (advanced) An advanced user writes their own SDK script locally — potentially with multiple files, helper modules, or vendored dependencies. They upload a tarball (`.tar.gz`) containing their script and any dependencies to the automation API. The platform stores this bundle and executes the entry point (`automation.py`) inside the sandbox at run time. This supports arbitrarily complex workflows: multi-turn conversations, conditional logic, multiple sequential agents, custom tool definitions, etc. — anything expressible in Python + the SDK. ### One executor for all three The platform doesn't distinguish between these at execution time. The executor receives SDK code (whether generated or user-authored), runs `python script.py` in a sandbox, and the conversation appears in the dashboard. ## 3\. Architecture Overview ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph sources["Event Sources (all webhooks)"] cron["⏰ Cron / Schedule"] github["GitHub App"] gitlab["GitLab"] future["Slack / Datadog / ..."] end subgraph routing["Webhook Endpoint"] auth["Authenticate via webhook_secret"] handler["Route by source_type → handler"] filter["Match event_filter against payload"] end subgraph queue["Message Queue"] mq[("Async, persistent queue")] end subgraph executor["SDK Code Executor"] exec["1. Create Cloud sandbox (repo + creds) 2. Extract tarball into sandbox 3. Set AUTOMATION_EVENT env var 4. python automation.py 5. SDK → Conversation on localhost 6. Conversation visible in Cloud dashboard"] end cron --> auth github --> auth gitlab --> auth future --> auth auth --> handler handler --> filter filter -->|match| mq mq --> exec ``` Key points: * **One executor.** It takes SDK code, runs it in a sandbox, and the conversation appears in the dashboard. Whether the trigger was a cron tick, a GitHub webhook, or a manual "Run Now" button — the path is the same. * **Async via message queue.** Event ingestion is fast and never drops. Execution is slow and resource-intensive. The queue decouples them, provides backpressure, and enables concurrency control. ## 4\. Prerequisites Two existing issues must be resolved first. They are independently valuable (they fix the SDK ↔ Cloud story) and are required for automations to work. ### 4a. Conversations visible in Cloud UI ([#13272]()) **Problem**: Conversations created via `OpenHandsCloudWorkspace` (the SDK path) don't appear in the Cloud dashboard. The Cloud only tracks conversations created through its own `app-conversations` API. **Why it blocks automations**: If automation-spawned conversations are invisible, there's no monitoring, no observability, no way to inspect what the agent did — which is the entire value prop of running on Cloud instead of GitHub Actions. ### 4b. SDK conversations inherit SaaS settings ([#13268]()) **Problem**: SDK-created sandboxes don't get the user's Cloud credentials (GitHub App token, LLM key), repo context, or setup scripts. Users must supply their own keys. **Why it blocks automations**: An automation's SDK script running in a Cloud sandbox needs the user's credentials. Users shouldn't have to hardcode API keys into their automation scripts — the platform should inject them, just like it does for interactive conversations. ### Sequencing These two are related: [APP-927](https://linear.app/all-hands-ai/issue/APP-927/bug-conversations-created-via-openhandscloudworkspace-post) is about **visibility**, [APP-924](https://linear.app/all-hands-ai/issue/APP-924/feat-sdk-created-conversations-should-inherit-saas-settings) is about **credentials and context**. The solution to [APP-927](https://linear.app/all-hands-ai/issue/APP-927/bug-conversations-created-via-openhandscloudworkspace-post) will inform [APP-924](https://linear.app/all-hands-ai/issue/APP-924/feat-sdk-created-conversations-should-inherit-saas-settings) (e.g., if conversations auto-register, credential injection can piggyback on the same mechanism). Both should be resolved before the automation system ships, though the automation data model and API can be built in parallel. ## 5\. Data Model ### Automation The core object. Defines *what* to run and *when*. | Field | Type | Description | | -- | -- | -- | | `id` | UUID | | | `user_id` | string | Owner | | `name` | string | e.g., "Weekly PR summary" | | `enabled` | bool | Toggle on/off | | `trigger` | AutomationTrigger | When to run (see below) | | `sdk_source` | blob | Tarball containing the SDK script + any dependencies | | `entry_point` | string | Script to execute, default `automation.py` | The `sdk_source` is always a tarball — even for prompt/plugin automations where the UI generates a single-file script. This keeps the storage model uniform and naturally supports the advanced case (multi-file bundles with dependencies). ### AutomationTrigger All triggers are fundamentally **webhook-based**. GitHub, GitLab, Slack, Jira, Datadog, cron — they all deliver events as webhook POSTs. The trigger definition is minimal: | Field | Type | Description | | -- | -- | -- | | `source_type` | string | `"github"`, `"gitlab"`, `"slack"`, `"cron"`, ... | | `webhook_secret` | string? | For authenticating inbound webhooks | | `event_filter` | dict? | Optional filter on the webhook payload (e.g., match on `event_type`, `repository`, `label`) | The webhook body carries all the details. The system routes events by `source_type` to the appropriate handler, and that handler knows how to interpret the payload and trigger an automation run. There is no special-casing at the data model level — cron is just another source type whose handler emits scheduled events. ### AutomationRun A record of each execution. Each run creates a real `AppConversation` visible in the Cloud dashboard. | Field | Type | Description | | -- | -- | -- | | `id` | UUID | | | `automation_id` | UUID | → Automation | | `conversation_id` | UUID? | → AppConversation (once started) | | `status` | enum | `PENDING` → `RUNNING` → `COMPLETED` / `FAILED` | | `started_at` | datetime | | ## 6\. API Surface Standard CRUD for `Automation` and `AutomationRun` resources, following existing app-server patterns (`app_conversation_router.py`, `sandbox_router.py`). Key additional endpoints: * **Inbound webhook**: a public URL per automation for receiving external events (GitHub, Slack, etc.), authenticated via webhook secret * **Manual run**: trigger an automation on demand ("Run Now") Detailed API design to be determined during implementation. ## 7\. Event Routing There is no separate "event" abstraction — the webhook payload **is** the event, and the `AutomationTrigger` on each automation defines how to match and handle it. ### Flow 1. Webhook arrives at the automation's endpoint 2. Authenticate via `webhook_secret` 3. Route by `source_type` to the appropriate handler 4. Handler evaluates `event_filter` against the payload — if it matches, enqueue for execution ### Message queue The handler enqueues matched events into a **message queue** rather than executing synchronously. This provides: * **Decoupling**: ingestion (fast, never drops) is separated from execution (slow, resource-intensive) * **Backpressure**: if many events arrive simultaneously (e.g., a batch of PRs), they queue up instead of overwhelming the executor * **Reliability**: events are persisted — if the executor crashes, events aren't lost The specific queue technology (Redis Streams, SQS, Celery, etc.) is an implementation decision — the key architectural point is that ingestion and execution are async and decoupled. ### Sources All sources — GitHub, GitLab, Slack, Datadog, cron — POST to the same type of webhook endpoint. The `source_type` on the trigger determines which handler interprets the payload. The Cloud's GitHub App already receives webhooks for installed repos; these can be routed through the same system. Cron is not special — a background scheduler just POSTs to the automation's webhook endpoint when it's time to run. ## 8\. SDK Code Execution When a run is dispatched: 1. **Create sandbox** via `POST /api/v1/sandboxes` — with repo context and credentials (requires [APP-924](https://linear.app/all-hands-ai/issue/APP-924/feat-sdk-created-conversations-should-inherit-saas-settings) to be resolved) 2. **Upload the SDK script** to the sandbox workspace (e.g., `/workspace/automation.py`) 3. **Template event variables** into the script (or pass them as environment variables so the script can read `os.environ["EVENT_PAYLOAD"]`) 4. **Execute**: `python /workspace/automation.py` 5. The SDK script creates `Agent`, `Conversation`, calls `run()` — all against the localhost agent-server inside the sandbox 6. **Conversation registration**: the conversation appears in Cloud dashboard (requires [APP-927](https://linear.app/all-hands-ai/issue/APP-927/bug-conversations-created-via-openhandscloudworkspace-post) to be resolved) 7. **Run status**: the executor monitors the script process and updates `AutomationRun.status` accordingly ### Trigger event data The platform sets an `AUTOMATION_EVENT` environment variable (JSON) inside the sandbox before executing the script. The SDK script reads it to get trigger-specific context: ```python # Inside the user's SDK script import os, json event = json.loads(os.environ.get("AUTOMATION_EVENT", "{}")) pr_number = event.get("pr_number") ``` ## 9\. Frontend The PRD ([PRD-88](https://linear.app/all-hands-ai/issue/PRD-88/prd-draft-scheduled-recurring-conversations)) specifies: * **Automation list view**: see all automations, their triggers, last run status, enable/disable toggle * **Create/edit form**: name, trigger configuration (cron builder or event selector), input (prompt text → generates code, plugin selector → generates code, or raw code editor) * **Run history**: for each automation, see past runs linked to their conversations * **"View Source" button**: shows the generated SDK code for prompt/plugin automations, allowing users to customize * **"Run Now" button**: manual trigger for any automation * **Copy-pasteable curl**: for webhook-triggered automations ## 10\. Phasing ### Phase 0: Prerequisites (parallel with Phase 1 data model work) * [APP-927](https://linear.app/all-hands-ai/issue/APP-927/bug-conversations-created-via-openhandscloudworkspace-post): SDK conversations visible in Cloud UI * [APP-924](https://linear.app/all-hands-ai/issue/APP-924/feat-sdk-created-conversations-should-inherit-saas-settings): SDK conversations inherit SaaS settings * These are independently valuable and unblock the PR review cloud mode too ### Phase 1: Scheduled conversations ([#12732]()) * `Automation`, `AutomationRun` data model + database migration * CRUD API for automations * Cron scheduler service * SDK code executor (sandbox creation + script upload + execution) * Basic UI: create automation with prompt or plugin, set cron schedule, view runs * **Proof point**: "Every Friday at 9am, summarize open PRs for repo X" ### Phase 2: GitHub event triggers * GitHub App webhook → event router * `GitHubEventTrigger` matching * Template PR/issue metadata as event variables * **Proof point**: PR review bot — configure "when PR labeled `review-this` → run PR review plugin" — no GitHub Action needed in target repo ### Phase 3: Generic webhooks + ecosystem * Inbound webhook endpoint with secret-based auth * Plugin marketplace integration (browse → install → configure as automation) * **Proof point**: Slack message triggers a conversation; Datadog alert triggers incident triage ## 11\. Open Questions All items below are open for team discussion. Preferences noted where applicable. 1. **Sandbox lifecycle**: Fresh sandbox per execution, or reuse a paused sandbox? *Preference*: fresh — simpler, safer, no state leakage between runs. 2. **Trigger event data**: When a webhook fires, how does the SDK script get the trigger payload (e.g., PR number, repo, branch)? *Preference*: platform sets an `AUTOMATION_EVENT` environment variable (JSON) inside the sandbox; script reads via `os.environ`. 3. **Plugins and extensions**: How does this relate to John Mason's extensions concept? *Preference*: if a plugin can be expressed as SDK code, it is naturally handled by this system — no separate execution path needed. 4. **Retry policy**: How should failed runs handle retries? *Preference*: user-configurable per automation, similar to GitHub Actions' YAML format (max retries, backoff strategy). 5. **Message queue technology**: Redis Streams, SQS, Celery, or something else? Depends on deployment topology and existing infrastructure. This also determines the cron implementation (e.g., Celery beat vs. a standalone scheduler process).
OpenHands/OpenHands#13275.title: [RFC] CLOUD - OpenHands Automations — Scheduled & Event-Driven Conversations

OpenHands/software-agent-sdk#2468.body:
## Bug Description

When a conversation enters an error state (e.g., due to an LLM API error like an oversized image rejection), the VSCode window becomes inaccessible — even though the underlying runtime/sandbox is still alive and functional.

This means the user loses access to their workspace editor precisely when they might need it most (to inspect files, manually fix issues, etc.).

## Reproduction Steps

1. Start a conversation with a running runtime (sandbox is alive, VSCode is accessible)
2. Trigger a conversation error — for example, send an oversized image that causes `litellm.BadRequestError` (see #2467)
3. The conversation enters an error state (red error banner shown in UI)
4. Try to open the VSCode window — it is no longer accessible

## Expected Behavior

The VSCode window should remain accessible as long as the runtime/sandbox is still running, regardless of the conversation's execution state. The conversation error state should not gate access to the workspace editor.

## Current Behavior

Once the conversation enters an error state, the VSCode window cannot be opened. The user has no way to access their workspace files through the editor, even though the sandbox is still alive.

## Context

The agent-server's `vscode_router.py` itself does not gate on conversation state — it simply serves the VSCode URL if the service is running. The issue likely originates in the cloud frontend or API layer which may block VSCode access based on the conversation's execution status.

This is especially problematic because:
- The runtime may still be perfectly healthy (it's an LLM error, not a sandbox error)
- The user may want to inspect or save their work after an error
- There's no workaround to access the workspace files
OpenHands/software-agent-sdk#2468.title: VSCode window inaccessible when conversation is in error state (runtime still alive)

## Describe your question

As you know conda is not quite free anymore. It's been like that for a couple of years now. They changed the licenses. 
The only channel which is still free is the `forge`.  However, I would exercise caution and state it very clearly that it may be problematic for some people who would like to use OpenDevin in their corporate context and they do not wish to pay for the subscriptions as they may be in the breach of the license.

## Additional context

Please read their T&C very carefully and reflect it somewhere in the docs, so it's clear for anyone.
Here is the relevant [section](https://legal.anaconda.com/policies/en/?name=terms-of-service#anaconda-terms-of-service)

BTW: Great project and a very impressive growth of this community! Well done :+1: All the best!

all-hands-ai/OpenHands#4281.body:
**Motivation**
Storing the token as a URI param will make it much more manageable and easier to access (React Router / Remix has a hook `useSearchParams` that can do just that) throughout the app rather than relying on local storage.

**Technical Design**
When a new session is created, store the ID as a parameter. Retrieving it is as easy as calling a hook within components, and React Router / Remix data loaders already allow for easy access to the URL and params.

**Alternatives to Consider**
Keep it in local storage

**Additional context**
Suggested by @tobitege
all-hands-ai/OpenHands#4281.title: Store session ID as a URI param instead of in-memory

all-hands-ai/OpenHands#8453.body:
**Problem Description**
Currently, OpenHands hardcodes the git user configuration to `user.name "openhands"` and `user.email "openhands@all-hands.dev"` in multiple places throughout the codebase. This prevents users from customizing the git author information for commits made by the agent.

**Current Hardcoded Locations**
- `openhands/runtime/action_execution_server.py` (lines 353-374)
- `openhands/resolver/send_pull_request.py` (lines 181-182)
- Various test files

**Proposed Solution**
Add configuration options to allow users to specify custom git username and email through:
1. Environment variables (e.g., `GIT_USER_NAME`, `GIT_USER_EMAIL`)
2. Configuration file options in `config.toml`
3. Fallback to current defaults if not specified

**Use Cases**
- Users want commits to be attributed to their actual identity
- Organizations want commits to use company email addresses
- Different projects may require different git configurations
- Compliance requirements for proper commit attribution

**Implementation Considerations**
- Should work across all runtime types (local, docker, remote)
- Should be consistent between resolver and runtime execution
- Should maintain backward compatibility with current defaults
- Should be documented in configuration documentation

**Related Code Paths**
- [Action Execution Server](https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/blob/main/openhands/runtime/action_execution_server.py#L353-374)
- [Pull Request Resolver](https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/blob/main/openhands/resolver/send_pull_request.py#L181-182)
- Configuration system in `openhands/core/config/`

**Original Issue Context**
This issue was originally about `setup.sh` not overriding hardcoded git config commands, but the core problem is that git configuration should be configurable through the standard OpenHands configuration system rather than being hardcoded.
all-hands-ai/OpenHands#8453.title: [Feature Request]: Make git username and email configurable

all-hands-ai/OpenHands#9655.body:
### Is there an existing issue for the same bug? (If one exists, thumbs up or comment on the issue instead).

- [x] I have checked the existing issues.

### Describe the bug and reproduction steps

litellm.BadRequestError: XaiException - Error code: 400 - {'code': 'Client specified an invalid argument', 'error': 'Argument not supported on this model: stop'}```

#### **Root Cause Analysis**

The error message explicitly states that the `stop` parameter is not supported by the target model. The xAI API documentation confirms this for Grok-4.

The issue stems from the `CodeActAgent`'s implementation, which appears to hardcode the addition of a `stop` sequence (e.g., `["</execute_bash>"]`) to every LLM API call. While this is a valid parameter for many models (like those from OpenAI and Anthropic), it causes a fatal API error when used with Grok-4, making the model unusable.

#### **Steps to Reproduce**

1.  Navigate to the OpenHands **Settings** page.
2.  Enable **Advanced** options and configure the LLM as follows:
    *   **Custom Model:** `xai/grok-4-0709`
    *   **Base URL:** `https://api.x.ai/v1`
    *   **API Key:** `[A valid xAI API key]`
3.  Save the settings and start a new agent session.
4.  Enter any initial prompt (e.g., "hello world").
5.  Observe that the agent immediately transitions to an `ERROR` state and the logs show the `BadRequestError` detailed above.

#### **Suggested Solution**

To resolve this and enable support for Grok-4, the agent code should be modified to conditionally add the `stop` parameter. It should only be included in the API call for models that are known to support it.

A simple and effective fix would be to check the model identifier before adding the parameter.

**Example (pseudo-code for `openhands/agenthub/codeact_agent/codeact_agent.py`):**

```python
# In the agent's step method, where the LLM call is prepared...
params = {
    "messages": messages,
    "temperature": 0.0,
    # other parameters...
}

# Conditionally add the 'stop' parameter
# self.llm.model should contain the model name, e.g., 'xai/grok-4-0709'
if "grok" not in self.llm.model:
    params["stop"] = self.stop_sequences # Or the hardcoded list

# Make the LLM call
response = self.llm.completion(**params)

### OpenHands Installation

Docker command in README

### OpenHands Version

_No response_

### Model Name

_No response_

### Operating System

None

### Logs, Errors, Screenshots, and Additional Context

_No response_
all-hands-ai/OpenHands#9655.title: [Bug]: CodeActAgent is incompatible with xAI Grok-4 due to hardcoded stop parameter

all-hands-ai/OpenHands#9670.body: **What problem or use case are you trying to solve?** We want better observability (the ability to answer new questions about the behavior of the system) while staying vendor neutral. We should be able to visualize log entries as associated with the larger events they are associated with, like conversations and agent actions. **Describe the UX or technical implementation you have in mind** https://opentelemetry.io/ Create a `monitoring` config section with an option to enable OpenTelemetry (aka OTel). When enabled, log statements go through that (in addition to current places) and there are "spans" annotated throughout the app to give them structure. People will configure the collector primarily through the standard OpenTelemetry environment variables. **Additional context** Observability #9579 Visualize Agent Loop #8916 ^ also included a nice graphic of what tracing enables. ## Alternatives## **JSON Logs**: A previous stop-gap solution was JSON Logs with metadata included in a useful subset. While non-standard this at least allowed custom scripts to get better picture of what's going on. To take this to the next level we would need to make it's presence in the codebase a lot more obtrusive and **LiteLLM Proxy**: We have recommended people use **LiteLLM Proxy** to serve their LLM-requests, which has several vendor integrations. This keeps does a good job at raw LLM requests but does not tie well to what the agent itself is doing. Again, to go further with it requires crossing into our codebase. **LogFire** is an opinionated OTel wrapper for Python by makers of Pydantic. It can ship to any backend, so one can argue that even though they are an O11y vendor, the SDK is still a vendor-neutral option. However, while trying it out in OpenHands I was prompted in the logs to sign-in to the LogFire website, and this is not what want to show our users. ### If you find this feature request or enhancement useful, make sure to add a 👍 to the issue
all-hands-ai/OpenHands#9670.title: Instrument OpenTelemetry

### Is there an existing issue for the same bug?

- [x] I have checked the existing issues.

### Describe the bug and reproduction steps

When running the OpenHands CLI, multiple SyntaxWarnings are displayed from the pydub library due to invalid escape sequences in regex patterns:

```
/Users/gneubig/.cache/uv/archive-v0/Jh3QtSaSzMrpYMUgnS8Px/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydub/utils.py:300: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\('
  m = re.match('([su]([0-9]{1,2})p?) \(([0-9]{1,2}) bit\)$', token)
/Users/gneubig/.cache/uv/archive-v0/Jh3QtSaSzMrpYMUgnS8Px/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydub/utils.py:301: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\('
  m2 = re.match('([su]([0-9]{1,2})p?)( \(default\))?$', token)
/Users/gneubig/.cache/uv/archive-v0/Jh3QtSaSzMrpYMUgnS8Px/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydub/utils.py:310: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\('
  elif re.match('(flt)p?( \(default\))?$', token):
/Users/gneubig/.cache/uv/archive-v0/Jh3QtSaSzMrpYMUgnS8Px/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydub/utils.py:314: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\('
  elif re.match('(dbl)p?( \(default\))?$', token):
```

### Expected behavior

No SyntaxWarnings should be displayed when running OpenHands CLI.

### Actual behavior

Multiple SyntaxWarnings are shown from the pydub dependency.

### OpenHands Installation

- [x] Docker
- [x] Development setup
- [x] pip
- [x] Other (please specify): uv

### OpenHands Version

Latest (main branch)

### Operating System

macOS

### Logs, Errors, Screenshots, and Additional Context

These warnings are coming from the pydub library (external dependency) and are related to invalid escape sequences in regex patterns. The warnings appear in pydub/utils.py at lines 300, 301, 310, and 314.

While these are warnings from an external dependency, they clutter the CLI output and should be addressed either by:
1. Updating to a newer version of pydub that fixes these warnings
2. Adding warning filters to suppress these specific warnings
3. Contributing a fix upstream to pydub

The warnings do not affect functionality but impact user experience by creating noisy output.

### Related Issues

These pydub SyntaxWarnings have been mentioned in other issues but were not the primary focus:
- #9192 - [Bug]: FieldOptions has no attribute RegisterExtension
- #9127 - [Bug]: Modal runtime fails with `Server disconnected without sending a response` (closed)

This issue is specifically dedicated to addressing the pydub SyntaxWarnings.
all-hands-ai/OpenHands#9682.title: [Bug]: pydub SyntaxWarnings for invalid escape sequences in regex patterns

### Describe the bug and reproduction steps

I encountered a few small issues related to the MCP settings UI.

~~1. If the user refreshes the MCP settings tab with F5, the page shows default config values (empty/default) instead of the actual saved config. Navigating to another tab and then back correctly loads the data.~~
~~2. The default MCP configuration is localized per language, which is unnecessary because it's a JSON value, and JSON can't have comments that could require localization.~~
~~3. Both the docs and the default values mention an shttp_servers key, but it's not present in the editor example nor in the localized defaults. Still, I've seen it appear, so I suspect the settings are modified at runtime. It either needs to be added as a valid key (and shown in the examples) or removed if unsupported. Also, if the MCP settings are indeed modified at runtime, I don't think that should happen, but if it's expected, then having a default value in the editor probably isn't necessary.~~
~~4. The docs linked in the settings page aren't very helpful: they only show config.toml examples.~~
~~5. I submitted a PR to improve the UI (#9771); however, having to still press two buttons to save, "Confirm Changes" (formerly "Apply Settings") and "Save Settings", is still a bit confusing. A cleaner solution might be needed (an unsaved changes modal prompt, maybe?).~~

Edit: Issues 1, 2 and 5 partially fixed by #9772.

all-hands-ai/OpenHands#9859.body:
## Problem

Currently, when users press the stop button during agent execution, the message displayed is not very user-friendly and can be confusing. The current message gives the impression that something went wrong or that work was lost.

## Proposed Solution

Change the message when the stop button is pressed to:

**"Stop button pressed. The action has not been executed."**

This message is:
- Clear and informative
- Explains what happened (user action)
- Clarifies the result (action not executed)
- Removes any ambiguity about whether work was lost

## Benefits

- Better user experience
- Clearer communication about what happened
- Reduces user confusion about the state of their work
- More professional and polished interface

## Context

This improvement was suggested during user testing where the current stop button message was found to be confusing and gave users the wrong impression about what had occurred.

all-hands-ai/OpenHands#9933.body:
## Bug Description
After following the setup instructions in `development.md`, the pre-commit hook fails when attempting to commit changes with the error:

```
husky - pre-commit script failed (code 127)
husky - command not found in PATH=...
```

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Follow the setup instructions in `development.md`
2. Run `make build` successfully
3. Create a new file and attempt to commit it: `git add newfile.txt && git commit -m "test"`
4. The commit fails with the pre-commit hook error

## Root Cause
The husky pre-commit hook in `frontend/.husky/pre-commit` calls `pre-commit` directly, but pre-commit is installed in the poetry virtual environment and not available in the system PATH.

## Expected Behavior
The pre-commit hook should run successfully and validate the code before committing.

## Environment
- OS: Linux (Ubuntu)
- Python: 3.12.3
- Poetry: 2.1.3
- Node.js: 22.17.1

## Solution
The fix is to change line 11 in `frontend/.husky/pre-commit` from:
```bash
pre-commit run --files openhands/**/* evaluation/**/* tests/**/* --show-diff-on-failure --config ./dev_config/python/.pre-commit-config.yaml
```

to:
```bash
poetry run pre-commit run --files openhands/**/* evaluation/**/* tests/**/* --show-diff-on-failure --config ./dev_config/python/.pre-commit-config.yaml
```

This ensures pre-commit runs within the poetry virtual environment where it is installed.
all-hands-ai/OpenHands#9933.title: Pre-commit hook fails with "command not found" error after following development.md setup

all-hands-ai/OpenHands#9982.body:
## Problem

The `make lint` command fails when run on a fresh clone of the repository because it does not ensure that the necessary prerequisites are installed before running the linters.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Clone the repository: `git clone https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands.git`
2. Navigate to the directory: `cd OpenHands`
3. Run: `make lint`

## Expected Behavior

The `make lint` command should work out of the box, automatically installing any missing dependencies before running the linters.

## Actual Behavior

The command fails with errors like:

```
sh: 1: react-router: not found
make[1]: *** [Makefile:197: lint-frontend] Error 127
make: *** [Makefile:200: lint] Error 2
```

## Root Cause

The `lint` target in the Makefile does not depend on the necessary setup steps:

- `lint-frontend` requires frontend dependencies to be installed (`npm install` in the frontend directory)
- `lint-backend` requires pre-commit hooks to be installed

Currently, these dependencies are only installed when running `make build`, but not when running `make lint` directly.

## Proposed Solution

Modify the Makefile to ensure that the `lint` targets have proper dependencies:

```makefile
lint-frontend: install-frontend-dependencies
	lint-backend: install-pre-commit-hooks
```

Or alternatively, create a `setup-lint` target that installs the minimal dependencies needed for linting:

```makefile
setup-lint:
	@$(MAKE) -s install-frontend-dependencies
	@$(MAKE) -s install-pre-commit-hooks

lint: setup-lint
	@$(MAKE) -s lint-frontend
	@$(MAKE) -s lint-backend
```

## Additional Context

This issue affects developer experience, as contributors might expect `make lint` to work immediately after cloning the repository, especially since it's a common command for code quality checks.

The `make build` command does work correctly and installs all necessary dependencies, but developers should not need to run a full build just to lint their code.
all-hands-ai/OpenHands#9982.title: `make lint` does not work out of the box - missing prerequisites

Currently, users can only configure git credentials (username and email) through backend configuration. There is no frontend UI to allow users to easily set their git credentials through the settings page.

## Proposed Solution
Add frontend settings UI components that allow users to:
- Configure git username

- Add git credential fields to frontend settings types
- Add UI components in the settings page
- Add translations for git settings
- Update settings save functionality to handle git credentials
- Ensure backend integration works properly

This would complement the existing backend git configuration functionality and make it more accessible to users.

all-hands-ai/OpenHands#10173.body:
### Is there an existing issue for the same bug? (If one exists, thumbs up or comment on the issue instead).

- [x] I have checked the existing issues.

### Describe the bug and reproduction steps

In the documentation and the `MCPConfig` class, we support loading the `[mcp]` section with content like below:

```toml
[mcp]
stdio_servers = [
  {
    name="Context7",
    command="npx",
    args=["-y", "@upstash/context7-mcp"],
  }
]
```

This is the required format, but currently in the CLI, we have 2 other methods which load and save the MCP settings to toml file in a format different from the expected one. Below is the config saved by the CLI:
```toml
[mcp]
[[mcp.stdio_servers]]
name = "Context7"
command = "npx"
args = [ "-y", "@upstash/context7-mcp",]
```

---
Root cause: The issue was in the `save_config_file` function in `openhands/cli/commands.py`. When the TOML library (`toml.dump()`) encounters arrays of dictionaries under a section, it automatically converts them to array-of-tables format instead of inline arrays.

all-hands-ai/OpenHands#10297.body:
## Problem

When users first access the OpenHands frontend, they may encounter 404 errors in the browser console due to i18n (internationalization) language code handling issues. This causes the first page load to fail in some cases.

## Error Details

The error appears as:
```
No routes matched location "/locales/en-US@posix/translation.json" 
ErrorResponseImpl {
  status: 404,
  statusText: 'Not Found',
  internal: true,
  data: 'Error: No route matches URL "/locales/en-US@posix/translation.json"',
  error: Error: No route matches URL "/locales/en-US@posix/translation.json"
      at getInternalRouterError (...)
      at Object.query (...)
      at handleDocumentRequest (...)
      ...
}
```

## Root Cause

The issue occurs when:
1. A user's browser has language settings with region-specific codes (e.g., `en-US@posix`, `en-GB`, `ja-JP`)
2. i18next tries to load translation files for these specific language codes
3. The application only has base language files (e.g., `/locales/en/translation.json`, `/locales/ja/translation.json`)
4. This results in 404 errors when requesting non-existent files like `/locales/en-US@posix/translation.json`

## Impact

- Users see console errors on first load
- In some cases, the first page load may fail entirely
- Poor user experience, especially for users with non-English browser settings

## Available Translation Files

The application currently provides translation files for:
- `ar`, `de`, `en`, `es`, `fr`, `it`, `ja`, `ko-KR`, `no`, `pt`, `tr`, `uk`, `zh-CN`, `zh-TW`

Note that some languages include region codes (`ko-KR`, `zh-CN`, `zh-TW`) while others are base languages only.

## Solution

The fix involves updating the i18next configuration to properly handle language code fallbacks, preventing 404 errors when region-specific language files don't exist.
all-hands-ai/OpenHands#10297.title: Frontend shows 404 errors on first load due to i18n language code handling

all-hands-ai/OpenHands#10350.body:
**What problem or use case are you trying to solve?**

The agent can continue a long-running command by sending an empty command "" in a `CmdRunAction`. However, when it is doing it three times in a role, the agent controller will flag it as agent-stuck-in-loop and stop the whole conversation.

**Describe the UX or technical implementation you have in mind**

We should:
1. The continue bash command shouldn't trigger a stuck in a loop error (e.g., add an exception in stuck in loop detection for empty command that resumes previous command)
2. (1) is probably a bad solution since it adds a new special case --- A better way would be, we should update the prompt here (https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/blob/e40681ca61fb2b4b602fe916d99def6b55b78d77/openhands/runtime/utils/bash.py#L550-L557) to tell the agent the previous command timed out AND the agent should try use the `timeout` argument in the `execute_bash` tool to run this command for longer time.

We should probably try (2) first, if it didn't solve the problem; Then we should try (1).

**Additional context**


### If you find this feature request or enhancement useful, make sure to add a 👍 to the issue

all-hands-ai/OpenHands#10386.body:
Implement a way to specify the base URL of the OpenHands instance under test, so E2E tests can run against localhost, CI, or remote instances.

What to cover
- Introduce a pytest option and/or environment variable (e.g., E2E_BASE_URL) used by tests/e2e/* instead of hardcoding http://localhost:12000.
- Ensure Playwright config or tests read from this value.

Acceptance Criteria
- All E2E tests can target arbitrary instances via configuration, defaulting to localhost.

Guidelines (must be followed in this issue):
1) Review existing end-to-end tests, especially tests/e2e/test_conversation.py and tests/e2e/test_settings.py for patterns.
2) Install pre-commit hooks and understand linting:
   - make install-pre-commit-hooks
   - Backend: pre-commit run --config ./dev_config/python/.pre-commit-config.yaml
   - Frontend (if touched): cd frontend && npm run lint:fix && npm run build ; cd ..
   - VS Code extension (if touched): cd openhands/integrations/vscode && npm run lint:fix && npm run compile ; cd ../../..
3) Implement a pytest-based end-to-end test under tests/e2e/ that exercises this functionality. Prefer pytest-playwright for UI flows; for CLI/headless flows, use subprocess and assert outputs. Add robust waits and screenshots like test_conversation.py. Include assertions that verify the intended behavior.
4) Lint and open a PR with the test code.
5) After opening the PR, wait 120 seconds, then check GitHub Actions workflow status. If any job fails, download logs locally and inspect. Re-read the relevant frontend/backend code to identify the cause and fix.
6) Repeat step 5 until all workflows pass (prioritize end-to-end tests first, then other workflows).
all-hands-ai/OpenHands#10386.title: E2E Infra: Support specifying the base URL for end-to-end tests

all-hands-ai/OpenHands#10462.body: **What problem or use case are you trying to solve?** In [https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/pull/9349](https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/pull/9349), we introduced a backend implementation for LLM security analyzer, which will predict `security_risk` for every agent action. We need to properly allow user to configure them and visualize the analyzer result in our frontend UI. **Describe the UX or technical implementation you have in mind** **Branch with current FE implementation - per** @xingyao I have OpenHands implemented a draft version here ([https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/tree/add-llm-risk-analyzer-w-fe](https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/tree/add-llm-risk-analyzer-w-fe)), but it sorts of conflicts with our existing UI/UX (i.e., i'm not sure if we still need to keep the security analyzer modal for invariant analyzer), so i figured it is probably better to create an issue first for discussion. **AC:** 1. Verify a user can enable the security risk analyzer in the LLM settings page 1. Move our "confirmation mode" out of "advanced llm settings" and into the main LLM page 2. Once "confirmation mode" is toggled on, we show the security analyzer drop-down 3. The drop down will by default sets to "LLM Analyzer (Default)", but use can still choose "Invariant Rule Analyzer" OR "None (Always ask for every action)" (basically set the security analyzer to `None`) 4. 2. Verify in the conversation UI, whenever there's a high-risk action, we will show a red warning above the confirmation button. Useful prompt for testing: `can you run three echo action, each with low, medium and high risk` 1. Verify a user is presented with a confirmation box to apporved actions considered risky 1. We will implement version 1 of these designs: [https://www.figma.com/design/iM5UBG1lnpg2sgE3cFnF1P/OpenHands?node-id=11684-18015&t=vXCXxIabWcM8DpZh-1](https://www.figma.com/design/iM5UBG1lnpg2sgE3cFnF1P/OpenHands?node-id=11684-18015&t=vXCXxIabWcM8DpZh-1) **Additional context** ### If you find this feature request or enhancement useful, make sure to add a 👍 to the issue
